docs.title: Welcome to Python Programming
docs.text:
Python is a popular programming language used to create websites, games, apps, data tools and artificial intelligence projects. It is known for being clear to read, powerful to use and a great first language for learning how programming works.

As you learn Python, you will practise giving instructions to the computer, storing information, making decisions and solving problems step by step.

docs.title: Python Outputs, Inputs and Variables
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Learn how to display outputs in Python.
  • Learn how to collect inputs from the user.
  • Understand how variables store data in a program.
  • Build simple Python programs using outputs, inputs and variables.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Use print() to display messages.
  • Use input() to ask the user questions.
  • Store information in variables.
  • Create a simple Python program using inputs and outputs.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Output Information displayed by a program. Input Information sent from the user to the computer. Variable A named storage place for data in a program. Value The data stored inside a variable.

docs.title: What is Python?
docs.text:
Python is a powerful programming language.

It is used to create:

  • Games
  • Websites
  • Apps
  • Data tools
  • Artificial intelligence programs

Python is popular because it is easier to read than many other programming languages.

docs.title: What is an Output?
docs.text:
An output is information displayed by the computer.

Programs use outputs to show messages, instructions, answers and results.

In Python, we display outputs using print().

Example:

print("Hello World")

This program displays the words:

Hello World

docs.title: What is an Input?
docs.text:
An input is information sent from the user to the computer.

Programs often need information from the user to work properly.

Examples of inputs:

  • Typing on a keyboard
  • Clicking a mouse
  • Speaking into a microphone
  • Tapping a touchscreen

In Python, we ask for inputs using input().

Example:

input("What is your name? ")

This program:

  1. Displays a question.
  2. Waits for the user to type an answer.

docs.title: What is a Variable?
docs.text:
A variable is a named storage place used to store data in a program.

Programs use variables to remember information.

Examples of information a program might store:

  • A player score in a game
  • A username
  • A password
  • A high score

Example:

name = "Mr Wood"

In this program:

Part Meaning name The variable name "Mr Wood" The value being stored

You can think of a variable like a labelled box that stores information.

docs.title: Combining Inputs and Variables
docs.text:
Inputs become much more useful when we store them in variables.

Example:

name = input("What is your name? ")
print(name)

What Happens Here?

  1. Python asks the user a question.
  2. The user types an answer.
  3. The answer is stored in the variable called name.
  4. The program displays the stored answer.

Programs become much more useful when we combine:

  • Inputs
  • Variables
  • Outputs

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a program that asks for the user's name and displays a greeting.

name = input("What is your name? ")

print("Hello", name)

Step-by-Step

  1. The program asks the user for their name.
  2. The answer is stored in the variable called name.
  3. The program displays a greeting.
  4. The stored name is displayed as part of the output.

docs.title: Programming Tasks
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1

Display the message:

Hello World

Task 2

Display three separate messages about yourself.

Your program should use three print() statements.

Task 3

Ask the user for their name.

Store their answer in a variable.

Display their name back to them.

Task 4

Ask the user for their favourite game.

Store their answer in a variable.

Display a sentence that includes their favourite game.

Task 5

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age

Store both answers in variables.

Display both pieces of information back to the user.

Task 6

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their favourite food
  • Their favourite colour

Display a short sentence using all three answers.

Task 7

Create a simple profile program.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age
  • Their favourite subject

Display the information as a short profile.

Task 8

Create a simple quiz question.

Ask the user a question and store their answer in a variable.

Display their answer back to them in a sentence.

Create a mini chatbot.

Your program should:

  • Ask the user at least four questions.
  • Store each answer in a variable.
  • Display a final message using the answers.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain the difference between an output, input and variable.
  • Use print() to display information.
  • Use input() to ask a question.
  • Store user input in a variable.
  • Combine inputs, variables and outputs in one program.

docs.title: Python Data Types and Arithmetic
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Understand the difference between strings, integers and real numbers.
  • Understand why computers need to know the type of data being stored.
  • Learn how to convert data from one type to another.
  • Use arithmetic operators to perform calculations in Python.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Identify whether data is a string, integer or real number.
  • Explain why data types are important.
  • Convert user input into an integer or real number.
  • Create Python programs that perform calculations.
  • Solve programming challenges using arithmetic operators.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Data Type The kind of data being stored, such as text or a number. String Text that is surrounded by speech marks. Integer A whole number. Float A number that contains a decimal point. Arithmetic Using maths operations such as add, subtract, multiply and divide. Convert Change data from one type into another type.

docs.title: Comments
docs.text:
Comments are notes written by programmers.

Python ignores comments when the program runs.

# This is a comment

Comments can be used to:

  • Explain code.
  • Leave reminders.
  • Make programs easier to understand.

docs.title: What is a Data Type?
docs.text:
A data type tells the computer what kind of information is being stored.

Different types of data are stored and used in different ways.

Data Type Meaning Examples Python Name String Text that must be surrounded by speech marks. name = "Dave" favourite_game = "Minecraft" str Integer A whole number that can be used in calculations. age = 14 score = 250 int Real Number A number that contains a decimal point. height = 1.72 price = 4.99 float

docs.title: Why Are Data Types Important?
docs.text:
Look at the following code:

num1 = input("Enter a number: ")
num2 = input("Enter another number: ")

answer = num1 + num2
print(answer)

If the user enters:

10
20

The output will be:

1020

This happens because Python treats inputs as strings.

Instead of adding the numbers together, it joins the text.

docs.title: Converting Data Types
docs.text:
We can convert data into an integer using int().

num1 = int(input("Enter a number: "))
num2 = int(input("Enter another number: "))

answer = num1 + num2
print(answer)

Output:

30

We can convert data into a real number using float().

temperature = float(input("Enter a temperature: "))

docs.title: Arithmetic Operators
docs.text: Operator Meaning Example + Add 5 + 2 = 7 - Subtract 5 - 2 = 3 * Multiply 5 * 2 = 10 / Divide 5 / 2 = 2.5

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a program that asks for two numbers and displays the total.

num1 = int(input("Enter the first number: "))
num2 = int(input("Enter the second number: "))

total = num1 + num2

print("The total is", total)

Step-by-Step

  1. The user enters two numbers.
  2. int() converts the inputs into integers.
  3. The numbers are added together.
  4. The answer is stored in a variable called total.
  5. The result is displayed.

docs.title: Programming Tasks
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Add the numbers together and display the answer.

Example output: The total is 15

Task 2

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Subtract the second number from the first number and display the answer.

Example output: The difference is 4

Task 3

Ask the user for two whole numbers.

Multiply the numbers together and display the answer.

Example output: The product is 24

Task 4

Ask the user for two numbers.

Divide the first number by the second number and display the answer.

Remember: Use float() if the answer might include a decimal.

Task 5

Ask the user for the length and width of a rectangle.

Calculate and display the area.

Formula: Area = Length x Width

Task 6

Ask the user for the price of an item and the quantity bought.

Display the total cost.

Formula: Total cost = Price x Quantity

Task 7

Create a calculator that asks the user for two numbers.

Calculate and display:

  • The total
  • The difference

Task 8

A cinema ticket costs £7.50.

Ask the user how many tickets they would like.

Calculate and display:

  • The ticket total
  • The final cost with a £1.50 booking fee added

A teacher enters three test scores.

Ask the user to enter all three scores.

Calculate and display:

  • Total score
  • Average score

Hint: Average = Total / 3

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain the difference between a string, integer and float.
  • Convert inputs using int() and float().
  • Use arithmetic operators correctly.
  • Complete all Level 4 tasks independently.

docs.title: Python Selection
docs.text:
Learning Intention Success Criteria
  • Remember how inputs, outputs, variables and data types are used in Python.
  • Understand how programs make decisions.
  • Learn how to use selection with if and else.
  • Use comparison operators to test conditions.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  • Write a program that makes a decision.
  • Use if and else correctly.
  • Convert user input into an integer before comparing numbers.
  • Indent code correctly inside selection statements.
  • Use comparison operators such as >, < and ==.

docs.title: Today's Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Selection A control structure that allows a program to make a decision. Condition A test that can be either true or false. If Runs code only when a condition is true. Else Runs code when the if condition is false. Comparison Operator A symbol used to compare two values. Indentation Spaces at the start of a line that show which code belongs inside a statement.

docs.title: What is Selection?
docs.text:
Selection is when a program makes a decision.

Programs often need to choose what to do next based on information they have been given.

Examples of selection:

  • If a player has no lives left, end the game.
  • If a password is correct, allow the user to log in.
  • If a customer is old enough, allow them to buy a ticket.
  • If a score is high enough, display a pass message.

In Python, selection is written using if and else.

docs.title: A Simple If Statement
docs.text:
An if statement checks a condition.

If the condition is true, the indented code runs.

age = 15

if age > 12:
    print("You can watch this film")

What Happens Here?

  1. The variable age stores the value 15.
  2. Python checks if age > 12.
  3. The condition is true.
  4. The message is displayed.

docs.title: If and Else
docs.text:
An else statement gives the program another option.

The else code runs when the if condition is false.

age = 10

if age > 12:
    print("You can watch this film")
else:
    print("You are not old enough")

What Happens Here?

  1. Python checks if age > 12.
  2. The condition is false because 10 is not greater than 12.
  3. The code inside else runs instead.

docs.title: Comparison Operators
docs.text:
Comparison operators are used to compare values.

Operator Meaning Example > Greater than age > 17 < Less than score < 50 == Equal to answer == "yes" >= Greater than or equal to age >= 18 <= Less than or equal to score <= 100 != Not equal to password != "python"

Important

Use one equals sign to store a value:

name = "Alex"

Use two equals signs to compare values:

if name == "Alex":
    print("Welcome Alex")

docs.title: Indentation and Colons
docs.text:
Python uses indentation to show which lines belong inside an if or else statement.

A colon : is needed at the end of the if and else lines.

age = int(input("What is your age? "))

if age > 17:
    print("You are old enough")
else:
    print("You are not old enough")

The indented lines are the instructions that run after the decision has been made.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Create a virtual barman program.

The program should ask the user for their age.

If the user is over 17, the program should display:

What can I get you?

Otherwise, the program should display:

You are not old enough.

age = int(input("What is your age? "))

if age > 17:
    print("What can I get you?")
else:
    print("You are not old enough.")

Step-by-Step

  1. The program asks the user for their age.
  2. int() converts the input into an integer.
  3. Python checks if the age is greater than 17.
  4. If the condition is true, the first message is displayed.
  5. If the condition is false, the else message is displayed.

docs.title: Programming Tasks
docs.text:
Start at Level 1 and go as far as you can!

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1

Ask the user for their age.

If their age is greater than 10, display:

You are older than 10.

Task 2

Ask the user for a number.

If the number is greater than 100, display:

That is a big number.

Task 3

Create a virtual barman program.

Ask the user for their age.

If they are over 17, display:

What can I get you?

Otherwise, display:

You are not old enough.

Task 4

Ask the user for a test score.

If the score is greater than or equal to 50, display:

You passed.

Otherwise, display:

Try again next time.

Task 5

Ask the user for a password.

If the password is equal to python, display:

Access granted.

Otherwise, display:

Access denied.

Task 6

Ask the user for their name.

If their name is equal to your name, display:

We have the same name.

Otherwise, display a greeting using their name.

Task 7

Create a cinema age checker.

Ask the user for their age.

If they are greater than or equal to 15, display:

You can watch this film.

Otherwise, display:

You are too young for this film.

Task 8

Ask the user for a number.

If the number is less than 0, display:

This is a negative number.

Otherwise, display:

This is zero or a positive number.

Create a simple quiz question.

Your program should:

  • Ask the user a question.
  • Store their answer in a variable.
  • Use selection to check whether the answer is correct.
  • Display a correct or incorrect message.
  • Use a comment to explain what your if statement does.

docs.title: Reflection
docs.text:
Before moving on, make sure you can:

  • Explain what selection means.
  • Use if and else in a Python program.
  • Convert input into an integer using int().
  • Use comparison operators correctly.
  • Explain why indentation and colons are important in selection.

docs.title: Next Level Python Tasks
docs.text:
These tasks combine the skills from:

  • Outputs, inputs and variables
  • Data types and arithmetic

Important Information before you Begin

You do not need to record your results for these tasks.

This is a test in resilience: try things, fix errors, and keep improving your code.

Remember, we learn through making mistakes.

docs.title: Rules for These Tasks
docs.text:
For every task:

  • Use clear variable names.
  • Ask the user for input where needed.
  • Convert numbers using int() or float().
  • Display answers with helpful messages.
  • Test your program with more than one set of inputs.

Important

These tasks only use the skills from lessons 1 and 2.

You do not need if, else or loops yet.

docs.title: Challenge Tasks
docs.text:
Start where you feel confident, then try the next level.

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1: Personal Receipt

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • The name of an item
  • The price of the item

Display a short receipt.

Example output: Alex bought a keyboard for £24.99

Task 2: Minutes Converter

Ask the user to enter a number of hours.

Convert the hours into minutes.

Formula: minutes = hours * 60

Example output: 3 hours is 180 minutes

Task 3: Game Score Total

Ask the user for:

  • Their player name
  • Their score in round 1
  • Their score in round 2
  • Their score in round 3

Calculate and display their total score.

Extension: Also display their average score.

Task 4: Shopping Basket

Ask the user for:

  • The price of item 1
  • The price of item 2
  • The price of item 3

Calculate and display the total cost.

Then add a delivery charge of 2.50.

Display the final amount to pay.

Task 5: Cinema Trip Calculator

A cinema ticket costs 7.50.

Popcorn costs 3.25.

A drink costs 2.10.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • How many tickets they want
  • How many popcorns they want
  • How many drinks they want

Calculate and display:

  • Ticket total
  • Snack total
  • Final total

Task 6: Room Paint Estimator

Ask the user for:

  • The width of a wall in metres
  • The height of a wall in metres
  • The cost of paint per square metre

Calculate and display:

  • The area of the wall
  • The total paint cost

Formula: area = width * height

Task 7: Fitness Tracker

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Steps walked on Monday
  • Steps walked on Tuesday
  • Steps walked on Wednesday
  • Steps walked on Thursday
  • Steps walked on Friday

Calculate and display:

  • Total steps
  • Average steps per day
  • Total distance walked

Use this estimate:

1 step = 0.0008 kilometres

Task 8: Party Planner

Ask the user for:

  • Number of guests
  • Cost of food per guest
  • Cost of drinks per guest
  • Room hire cost

Calculate and display:

  • Total food cost
  • Total drinks cost
  • Room hire cost
  • Final party cost
  • Cost per guest

Task 9: Mini Budget Planner

Create a program that helps someone plan a weekly budget.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Weekly income
  • Food cost
  • Travel cost
  • Entertainment cost
  • Savings amount

Calculate and display:

  • Total spending
  • Money left after spending
  • Money left after spending and savings
  • A clear budget summary using the user's name

Extra challenge: Ask for one more spending category of your choice and include it in the calculations.

Task 10: Theme Park Cost Calculator

A group is visiting a theme park.

Ask the user for:

  • Group name
  • Number of child tickets
  • Number of adult tickets
  • Number of meal deals
  • Travel cost

Use these prices:

Item Price Child ticket 14.50 Adult ticket 22.00 Meal deal 6.75

Calculate and display:

  • Child ticket total
  • Adult ticket total
  • Meal deal total
  • Travel cost
  • Final group cost
  • Average cost per person

docs.title: Challenge Tips
docs.text:
• Build one part at a time.
  • Test each calculation before adding the next one.
  • Use float() for prices and measurements.
  • Use int() for whole numbers such as people, tickets or scores.
  • Use comments to explain important parts of your code.

docs.title: Next Level Selection Tasks
docs.text:
These tasks combine the skills from:

  • Outputs, inputs and variables
  • Data types and arithmetic
  • Selection with if and else

Important information before you begin.........

You do not need to record your results for these tasks.

This is a test in resilience: try things, fix errors, and keep improving your code.

Remember, we learn through making mistakes.

docs.title: Rules for These Tasks
docs.text:
For every task:

  • Use clear variable names.
  • Ask the user for input where needed.
  • Convert numbers using int() or float().
  • Use if and else to make decisions.
  • Display clear messages for both possible outcomes.
  • Test your program with values that make each outcome happen.

Important

These tasks use the skills from lessons 1, 2 and 3.

You should use if, else, comparison operators and correct indentation.

docs.title: Challenge Tasks
docs.text:
Start where you feel confident, then try the next level.

Level 1Level 2Level 3Level 4Challenge

Task 1: Age Checker

Ask the user for their age.

If they are 12 or older, display:

You can join the club.

Otherwise, display:

You are not old enough yet.

Task 2: High Score Checker

Ask the user for their game score.

If the score is greater than 1000, display:

High score achieved!

Otherwise, display:

Keep practising.

Task 3: Password Checker

Ask the user to enter a password.

If the password is equal to python, display:

Access granted.

Otherwise, display:

Access denied.

Task 4: Temperature Warning

Ask the user to enter a temperature.

If the temperature is less than 0, display:

Warning: freezing conditions.

Otherwise, display:

Temperature is above freezing.

Task 5: Cinema Ticket Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age

If they are 15 or older, display a message saying they can watch the film.

Otherwise, display a message saying they are too young.

Your message should include their name.

Task 6: Test Result

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their test score
  • The maximum possible score

Calculate their percentage.

If the percentage is greater than or equal to 50, display a pass message.

Otherwise, display a try again message.

Formula: percentage = score / maximum * 100

Task 7: Discount Calculator

Ask the user for:

  • The price of an item
  • Their age

If the user is under 16, they receive a 20% discount.

Otherwise, they pay the full price.

Display:

  • The original price
  • The discount amount
  • The final price

Hint: discount = price * 0.20

Task 8: Delivery Charge

Ask the user for the total cost of their shopping basket.

If the total is greater than or equal to 25, delivery is free.

Otherwise, add a delivery charge of 3.99.

Display:

  • Basket total
  • Delivery cost
  • Final amount to pay

Task 9: Simple Login and Spend Checker

Ask the user for:

  • Their username
  • Their password
  • The amount they want to spend

If the password is equal to python123, display a welcome message using their username.

Otherwise, display an access denied message.

Then create a second decision:

If the amount they want to spend is greater than 50, display:

Manager approval needed.

Otherwise, display:

Purchase approved.

Task 10: Event Ticket Program

Create a program for an event ticket desk.

Ask the user for:

  • Their name
  • Their age
  • How many tickets they want

A ticket costs 8.50.

Calculate the ticket total.

If the user is 13 or older, display:

  • A message saying they can attend
  • Their name
  • Number of tickets
  • Total cost

Otherwise, display a message saying they must attend with an adult.

Extra challenge: Add a booking fee of 1.50 to the total cost.

docs.title: Challenge Tips
docs.text:
• Write the input lines first.
  • Convert numeric inputs before comparing them.
  • Remember that = stores a value and == compares a value.
  • Put a colon : after every if and else.
  • Indent the code that belongs inside each decision.
  • Test each task twice: once for the if outcome and once for the else outcome.

docs.title: Python Programming Assessment
docs.text:
This assessment checks the skills you have learned in the Python basics unit.

You will use:

  • Outputs
  • Inputs
  • Variables
  • Data types
  • Arithmetic
  • Selection with if and else

docs.title: Assessment Levels
docs.text:
Each level becomes more challenging.

Start at the level your teacher tells you to complete.

Level Main Skills Level 1 Read, correct and explain simple Python code. Level 2 Create simple input, storage and output programs. Level 3 Use selection to compare numbers and test scores. Level 4 Build a more challenging program that orders three numbers.

docs.title: What to Submit
docs.text:
For each program task, submit evidence of your code.

Your teacher may ask you to:

  • Paste your code into your work.
  • Add a screenshot of your code.
  • Add a screenshot of your program running.
  • Explain what your code does.

Screenshot Reminder

Make sure screenshots are cropped and large enough to read clearly.

docs.title: Assessment Tips
docs.text:
• Read each task carefully before writing code.
  • Use clear variable names.
  • Convert number inputs using int() or float().
  • Remember that = stores a value and == compares a value.
  • Test your program with more than one input.
  • Fix errors one at a time.

docs.title: Assessment Level 1
docs.text: Level 1 checks that you can read, correct and explain simple Python code.

docs.title: Task 1: Fix the Input
docs.text:
The code below is meant to ask the user for their name.

name = input("What is your name?)

Write the corrected version.

docs.title: Task 2: Convert a Number
docs.text:
The code below is meant to convert a variable called number into an integer.

number = int(number)

Explain what this line of code does.

docs.title: Task 3: Output a Message
docs.text:
The code below displays a message.

print("Hello World")

Explain what will appear on the screen.

docs.title: Task 4: Read the Selection
docs.text:
Look at the code below.

if mood == "good":
    print("You're happy")
else:
    print("You're not happy")

Answer these questions:

  1. What condition is being checked?
  2. What is displayed if mood is equal to "good"?
  3. What is displayed if mood is not equal to "good"?
  4. Why is the code under if and else indented?

docs.title: Assessment Level 2
docs.text: Level 2 checks that you can use inputs, variables and outputs in short programs.

docs.title: Task 1: Favourite Film
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in their favourite film.
  • Stores the answer in a variable.
  • Displays the film name back to the user in a sentence.

Your output should include:

I'd give it 4 stars

Example:

I watched Inside Out and I'd give it 4 stars.

docs.title: Task 2: Explain the Code
docs.text:
Look at this line of code:

item = input("What is your favourite film? ")

Explain what the line does.

Your answer should mention:

  • The question shown to the user.
  • The user's answer.
  • The variable used to store the answer.

docs.title: Task 3: Happy Checker
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user if they are happy.
  • Tells the user to answer yes or no.
  • Stores their answer in a variable.

If the user enters yes, display:

Glad you are happy!

Otherwise, display:

What's up?

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Assessment Level 3
docs.text: Level 3 checks that you can use selection with numbers.

docs.title: Task 1: Largest Number
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in the first number.
  • Asks the user to type in the second number.
  • Stores both numbers in variables.
  • Decides which number is larger.
  • Displays the largest number on the screen.

Remember to convert both inputs into integers.

Example:

Enter the first number: 12
Enter the second number: 7
The largest number is 12

docs.title: Task 2: Test Score
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in their test score out of 100.
  • Stores the score in a variable.
  • Displays YOU PASSED!! if the score is 50 or greater.
  • Displays YOU FAILED!! if the score is less than 50.

Example:

Enter your score: 62
YOU PASSED!!

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code for each task.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Hints
docs.text:
• Use int(input(...)) for number inputs.
  • Use > or >= to compare numbers.
  • Use if and else.
  • Check your indentation carefully.

docs.title: Assessment Level 4
docs.text: Level 4 checks that you can combine inputs, variables, number conversion and selection to solve a more challenging problem.

docs.title: Task: Order Three Numbers
docs.text:
Create a program that:

  • Asks the user to type in three numbers.
  • Stores each number in a variable.
  • Displays the three numbers in order.
  • Starts with the smallest number.

Example:

Enter the first number: 8
Enter the second number: 3
Enter the third number: 12
The numbers in order are 3, 8, 12

docs.title: Your Program Must
docs.text:
• Use three separate inputs.
  • Convert each input into an integer.
  • Use selection to compare the numbers.
  • Display the numbers from smallest to largest.
  • Work with different sets of numbers.

docs.title: Test Data
docs.text:
Test your program with at least three different sets of numbers.

Test Numbers Entered Expected Order 1 8, 3, 12 3, 8, 12 2 20, 5, 10 5, 10, 20 3 1, 9, 4 1, 4, 9

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
Submit a screenshot or copy of your code.

Make sure it is cropped and large enough to read.

docs.title: Hints
docs.text:
• Solve one comparison at a time.
  • Think carefully about every possible order.
  • Test your program after each change.
  • Check that your indentation matches your if and else statements.

docs.title: Godot 4.5 Beginner Tutorial Lessons
docs.text:
This 21 lesson sequence is based on the 8-video Godot 4.5 beginner playlist by Godot Dev Checkpoint:

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLVzjdZVCXNTz6ZCs_YcXTlXCZYzTOk-Q3

The playlist builds a small 2D platform game while introducing the Godot editor, scenes, nodes, GDScript, movement, input actions, jumping, reusable coin pickups, signals, UI buttons, win conditions and scene resets.

Each lesson is designed for a 45 minute classroom slot.

docs.title: Source Videos
docs.text: Video Title 1 Godot 4.5 Basics: Setup, Nodes, Scenes, & The Editor Explained! 2 Your First Code! Hello World in GDScript 3 Basic 2D Movement! Making a Character Walk 4 Custom Controls! Add WASD & Input Actions Setup 5 Gravity & Jumping! Making a Platformer Character 6 Reusable Scene! Creating a Coin Pickup 7 Node Communication! Using Signals for UI Interaction 8 Complete Game Loop! Win Condition, End Screen, & Scene Reset

docs.title: Lesson Sequence
docs.text: Lesson Topic Source Video 1 Godot setup and editor tour Video 1 2 Scenes, nodes and first scene Video 1 3 First GDScript and Hello World Video 2 4 Variables, functions and script structure Video 2 5 CharacterBody2D and physics basics Video 3 6 Horizontal player movement Video 3 7 Input Map and WASD controls Video 4 8 Custom controls and movement polish Video 4 9 Gravity, jumping and Vector2 Video 5 10 Platformer feel and movement tuning Video 5 11 Reusable scenes and instancing Video 6 12 Area2D coin pickups Video 6 13 Signals and score system Video 6 14 UI buttons and interaction Video 7 15 Node communication and decoupling Video 7 16 Game state and win condition Video 8 17 End screen and scene reset Video 8 18 Complete game loop Video 8 19 Debugging and refinement Whole playlist 20 Level design and extension Whole playlist 21 Showcase and export preparation Whole playlist

docs.title: Lesson 1 - Godot Setup and Editor Tour
docs.text: Source video: Godot 4.5 Basics: Setup, Nodes, Scenes, & The Editor Explained!

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Learn what Godot is, create a new project and identify the main areas of the editor.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can create a Godot project.
  • I can identify the Scene, FileSystem, Inspector and viewport panels.
  • I can save a project in an organised folder.
  • I can explain what a game engine does.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Godot A free game engine used to make 2D and 3D games. Project The folder containing all files for a game. Editor The main Godot workspace. Viewport The area where scenes are viewed and edited.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: name three things a game engine might help with. 10 mins Teacher demo: create a Godot 4.5 project and tour the interface. 10 mins Guided task: students create and save their own project. 15 mins Independent task: label the editor areas and explore safe settings. 5 mins Plenary: students explain one editor panel.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create a project called FirstPlatformer.
  2. Locate the Scene panel.
  3. Locate the FileSystem panel.
  4. Locate the Inspector.
  5. Write one sentence explaining what each panel is for.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is it important to keep all project files inside the project folder?

docs.title: Lesson 2 - Scenes, Nodes and First Scene
docs.text: Source video: Godot 4.5 Basics: Setup, Nodes, Scenes, & The Editor Explained!

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Understand how Godot games are built from scenes and nodes.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can create a new scene.
  • I can add nodes to a scene.
  • I can rename nodes clearly.
  • I can save and run a basic scene.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Scene A reusable part of a game, such as a level, player or menu. Node A building block with a specific job. Root node The top node in a scene. Child node A node placed underneath another node.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: sort examples into possible scenes and nodes. 10 mins Teacher demo: create a simple 2D scene. 10 mins Guided task: add and rename nodes. 15 mins Independent task: build a simple test scene. 5 mins Plenary: explain scene vs node.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create a new 2D scene.
  2. Add a visible object such as a Sprite2D or ColorRect.
  3. Rename the root node clearly.
  4. Save the scene as main.tscn.
  5. Run the scene and check it appears.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why might a player be saved as its own scene?

docs.title: Lesson 3 - First GDScript and Hello World
docs.text: Source video: Your First Code! Hello World in GDScript

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Write and run the first GDScript program in Godot.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can attach a script to a node.
  • I can use _ready().
  • I can use print() to test code.
  • I can read the Output panel.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning GDScript Godot's built-in scripting language. Script A file containing code attached to a node. Function A named block of code. Output The panel where printed messages appear.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: predict what print("Hello") will do. 10 mins Teacher demo: attach a script and use _ready(). 10 mins Guided task: print several test messages. 15 mins Independent task: customise messages and fix errors. 5 mins Plenary: explain when _ready() runs.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
extends Node2D

func _ready():
    print("Hello Godot")

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Attach a script to the main scene.
  2. Print Hello Godot.
  3. Print your name.
  4. Add a second line of output.
  5. Break one line, read the error, then fix it.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What is the purpose of the Output panel?

docs.title: Lesson 4 - Variables, Functions and Script Structure
docs.text: Source video: Your First Code! Hello World in GDScript

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Use variables and functions to organise simple GDScript code.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can create a variable.
  • I can update a variable.
  • I can write a custom function.
  • I can call a function from _ready().

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Variable A named place to store a value. Value The data stored in a variable. Function call Running a function by using its name. Indentation Spacing that shows which code belongs together.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: identify variable names and values. 10 mins Teacher demo: variables and custom functions. 10 mins Guided task: create a score variable. 15 mins Independent task: add messages and variable updates. 5 mins Plenary: explain why indentation matters.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
extends Node2D

var score = 0

func _ready():
    add_point()
    print(score)

func add_point():
    score = score + 1

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create a variable called score.
  2. Print the starting score.
  3. Create a function called add_point.
  4. Make the function increase the score.
  5. Call the function twice and predict the output.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why are functions useful when a game becomes larger?

docs.title: Lesson 5 - CharacterBody2D and Physics Basics
docs.text: Source video: Basic 2D Movement! Making a Character Walk

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Create a basic 2D player scene using CharacterBody2D.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can create a player scene.
  • I can add a collision shape.
  • I can explain why physics bodies need collision.
  • I can use _physics_process().

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning CharacterBody2D A node used for controllable 2D characters. CollisionShape2D A shape that defines physical boundaries. Physics Rules for movement and collisions. _physics_process() A function that runs regularly for physics updates.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: what does a platform character need to work? 10 mins Teacher demo: create a player scene. 10 mins Guided task: add visible body and collision. 15 mins Independent task: test and fix missing collision warnings. 5 mins Plenary: explain why collision shapes matter.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create player.tscn.
  2. Add CharacterBody2D as the root node.
  3. Add a visible child node.
  4. Add a CollisionShape2D.
  5. Attach a script with _physics_process(delta).

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What problem happens if a physics body has no collision shape?

docs.title: Lesson 6 - Horizontal Player Movement
docs.text: Source video: Basic 2D Movement! Making a Character Walk

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Make a 2D player move left and right using keyboard input.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can check keyboard input.
  • I can change velocity.
  • I can use move_and_slide().
  • I can tune movement speed.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Input A key, button or action controlled by the player. Velocity Speed and direction of movement. move_and_slide() A Godot method used to move a character body. Speed How fast an object moves.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: predict direction from positive and negative values. 10 mins Teacher demo: movement code structure. 10 mins Guided task: move left and right. 15 mins Independent task: tune speed and test behaviour. 5 mins Plenary: explain velocity.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
extends CharacterBody2D

var speed = 200

func _physics_process(delta):
    var direction = Input.get_axis("ui_left", "ui_right")
    velocity.x = direction * speed
    move_and_slide()

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add a speed variable.
  2. Read left and right input.
  3. Set velocity.x.
  4. Call move_and_slide().
  5. Try three different speed values.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is velocity.x used for left and right movement?

docs.title: Lesson 7 - Input Map and WASD Controls
docs.text: Source video: Custom Controls! Add WASD & Input Actions Setup

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Use Godot's Input Map to create custom movement actions.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can open the Input Map.
  • I can create custom actions.
  • I can bind keys to actions.
  • I can update code to use custom actions.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Input Map Godot settings where input actions are created. Action A named control such as move_left. Bind Connect a key or button to an action. WASD Common keyboard controls for movement.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: list keyboard controls used in games. 10 mins Teacher demo: create Input Map actions. 10 mins Guided task: add WASD controls. 15 mins Independent task: update movement code. 5 mins Plenary: explain why actions beat hard-coded keys.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create move_left.
  2. Create move_right.
  3. Bind A and D.
  4. Keep arrow keys working too.
  5. Update code to use the new action names.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is an action name easier to manage than checking a specific key?

docs.title: Lesson 8 - Custom Controls and Movement Polish
docs.text: Source video: Custom Controls! Add WASD & Input Actions Setup

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Improve player control by testing input actions and refining movement behaviour.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can test multiple control schemes.
  • I can spot input mistakes.
  • I can make movement stop correctly.
  • I can comment code clearly.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Control scheme The set of inputs used to play a game. Debug Find and fix problems. Comment A note in code for humans. Responsiveness How quickly controls feel like they react.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: what makes controls feel good or bad? 10 mins Teacher demo: test and debug input actions. 10 mins Guided task: improve movement stopping. 15 mins Independent task: playtest and record issues. 5 mins Plenary: one control improvement made today.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Test A/D controls.
  2. Test arrow key controls.
  3. Check the player stops when no key is pressed.
  4. Add a short comment above the movement code.
  5. Ask another student to playtest and give feedback.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What is one sign that controls are not responsive enough?

docs.title: Lesson 9 - Gravity, Jumping and Vector2
docs.text: Source video: Gravity & Jumping! Making a Platformer Character

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Add gravity and jumping to a 2D platformer character.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can explain what gravity does in a platform game.
  • I can use is_on_floor().
  • I can apply jump velocity.
  • I can describe Vector2 as x and y movement.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Gravity A force that pulls the character downward. Jump velocity The upward movement applied when jumping. Vector2 A pair of x and y values. Floor check Testing whether the character is touching the ground.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: sketch x and y directions on screen. 10 mins Teacher demo: gravity and floor checks. 10 mins Guided task: add jump input. 15 mins Independent task: tune gravity and jump strength. 5 mins Plenary: explain why jump velocity is negative.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
extends CharacterBody2D

var speed = 200
var jump_velocity = -350
var gravity = 900

func _physics_process(delta):
    if not is_on_floor():
        velocity.y += gravity * delta

    if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and is_on_floor():
        velocity.y = jump_velocity

    move_and_slide()

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add a jump input action.
  2. Add a gravity variable.
  3. Apply gravity when the player is not on the floor.
  4. Make the player jump only from the floor.
  5. Tune jump height.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why should the player only jump when is_on_floor() is true?

docs.title: Lesson 10 - Platformer Feel and Movement Tuning
docs.text: Source video: Gravity & Jumping! Making a Platformer Character

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Refine the movement values so the player character feels better to control.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can test movement values systematically.
  • I can adjust speed, gravity and jump strength.
  • I can describe how values affect game feel.
  • I can record settings that work well.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Game feel How satisfying and responsive a game feels to play. Tuning Adjusting values to improve behaviour. Playtest Try a game to find what works and what needs changing. Iteration Making repeated improvements.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: describe a game with good movement. 10 mins Teacher demo: tune one movement variable at a time. 10 mins Guided task: create a testing table. 15 mins Independent task: playtest three movement setups. 5 mins Plenary: justify chosen movement values.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Test a slow speed value.
  2. Test a fast speed value.
  3. Test two gravity values.
  4. Test two jump values.
  5. Record the best combination and explain why.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why should you change one movement value at a time?

docs.title: Lesson 11 - Reusable Scenes and Instancing
docs.text: Source video: Reusable Scene! Creating a Coin Pickup

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Create a reusable coin scene that can be placed multiple times in a level.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can create a separate coin scene.
  • I can instance a scene into another scene.
  • I can explain why reusable scenes save time.
  • I can organise scene files clearly.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Instance A copy of a scene placed inside another scene. Reusable Designed so it can be used more than once. Packed scene A saved scene that can be instanced. Level The game area where the player moves.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: list game objects that appear many times. 10 mins Teacher demo: create and save coin.tscn. 10 mins Guided task: instance coins in the level. 15 mins Independent task: arrange a simple collection route. 5 mins Plenary: scene reuse explanation.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create a new coin scene.
  2. Add a visible coin shape or sprite.
  3. Save it as coin.tscn.
  4. Instance the coin in the main scene.
  5. Place at least five coins around the level.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is it better to edit one reusable coin scene than five separate coin objects?

docs.title: Lesson 12 - Area2D Coin Pickups
docs.text: Source video: Reusable Scene! Creating a Coin Pickup

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Use Area2D to detect when the player collects a coin.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can add Area2D to a pickup.
  • I can add a collision shape for detection.
  • I can connect a body entered signal.
  • I can remove a collected coin.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Area2D A node used to detect when objects enter an area. Detection Noticing when one object enters another object's area. Signal A message emitted when something happens. queue_free() Removes a node safely from the scene.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: what should happen when a player touches a coin? 10 mins Teacher demo: Area2D and collision shape. 10 mins Guided task: connect detection signal. 15 mins Independent task: make coins disappear when collected. 5 mins Plenary: explain Area2D vs CharacterBody2D.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
extends Area2D

func _on_body_entered(body):
    queue_free()

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Make the coin root an Area2D.
  2. Add a CollisionShape2D.
  3. Connect body_entered.
  4. Use queue_free() when collected.
  5. Test all coin instances.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why does a pickup use detection rather than platform collision?

docs.title: Lesson 13 - Signals and Score System
docs.text: Source video: Reusable Scene! Creating a Coin Pickup

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Use signals to notify the game when coins are collected and update a score.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can describe what a signal does.
  • I can emit a custom signal.
  • I can update a score variable.
  • I can test whether each coin increases the score.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Emit Send out a signal. Listener Code that responds to a signal. Score A value that tracks progress or points. Custom signal A signal created by the programmer.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: draw a message from coin to game manager. 10 mins Teacher demo: custom signal from coin. 10 mins Guided task: connect signal to score code. 15 mins Independent task: display and test score changes. 5 mins Plenary: explain why signals reduce dependency.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
signal collected

func _on_body_entered(body):
    collected.emit()
    queue_free()

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add a custom collected signal to the coin.
  2. Emit the signal when the player enters.
  3. Create a score variable in the main scene.
  4. Increase score when a coin is collected.
  5. Print or display the updated score.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why might a coin send a signal instead of directly changing the score itself?

docs.title: Lesson 14 - UI Buttons and Interaction
docs.text: Source video: Node Communication! Using Signals for UI Interaction

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Create simple user interface elements and respond to button presses.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can add a UI button.
  • I can connect the button's pressed signal.
  • I can run code when a button is clicked.
  • I can place UI elements clearly.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning UI User interface: buttons, labels and menus. Button A UI node the player can click. Pressed signal A signal emitted when a button is clicked. Control node A Godot node used for interface layouts.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: list UI elements in games. 10 mins Teacher demo: add a button and connect pressed. 10 mins Guided task: print a message on click. 15 mins Independent task: create a small menu panel. 5 mins Plenary: explain event-driven code.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add a Button node.
  2. Change the button text.
  3. Connect the pressed signal.
  4. Print a message when clicked.
  5. Add a Label that could show instructions or score.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What event causes a button's pressed signal?

docs.title: Lesson 15 - Node Communication and Decoupling
docs.text: Source video: Node Communication! Using Signals for UI Interaction

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Use signals to help nodes communicate without becoming tightly connected.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can explain node communication.
  • I can connect a signal between nodes.
  • I can update UI from game state.
  • I can explain decoupling in simple terms.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Communication One node sending information to another. Decoupling Keeping nodes independent where possible. Game state Important values such as score, lives or win status. Callback A function that runs in response to an event.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: identify which nodes need to communicate. 10 mins Teacher demo: connect game events to UI. 10 mins Guided task: update a label from a signal. 15 mins Independent task: improve score or message UI. 5 mins Plenary: explain decoupling with the coin example.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add a score Label.
  2. Update the Label when score changes.
  3. Use a signal where possible.
  4. Add a message Label for game feedback.
  5. Test that UI changes without breaking coin pickup code.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is it useful when the coin does not need to know exactly how the UI works?

docs.title: Lesson 16 - Game State and Win Condition
docs.text: Source video: Complete Game Loop! Win Condition, End Screen, & Scene Reset

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Create a win condition that checks whether the player has collected enough coins.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can track total score or coins collected.
  • I can compare the score with a target.
  • I can trigger a win state.
  • I can prevent the win logic from running too early.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Win condition The rule that decides when the player wins. Target The amount needed to complete the objective. Boolean A true or false value. State The current condition of the game.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: define winning for different games. 10 mins Teacher demo: score target and win check. 10 mins Guided task: add a has_won Boolean. 15 mins Independent task: test win condition with coins. 5 mins Plenary: explain how the game knows it is complete.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
var score = 0
var target_score = 5
var has_won = false

func add_score():
    score += 1
    if score >= target_score and not has_won:
        has_won = true
        print("You win!")

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Set a target number of coins.
  2. Check score after each pickup.
  3. Print a win message.
  4. Add a Boolean to stop repeated win messages.
  5. Test winning with different target values.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why is a has_won Boolean useful?

docs.title: Lesson 17 - End Screen and Scene Reset
docs.text: Source video: Complete Game Loop! Win Condition, End Screen, & Scene Reset

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Show an end screen when the player wins and allow the scene to restart.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can show or hide a UI panel.
  • I can connect a restart button.
  • I can reload the current scene.
  • I can test the game from start to finish.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning End screen A screen shown after the objective is complete. Reset Start the scene again. Visible Whether a node can be seen. Reload Load the current scene again from the beginning.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: what should happen after the player wins? 10 mins Teacher demo: end panel and restart button. 10 mins Guided task: show panel on win. 15 mins Independent task: restart the scene. 5 mins Plenary: describe the complete player journey.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
func show_end_screen():
    $EndScreen.visible = true

func _on_restart_button_pressed():
    get_tree().reload_current_scene()

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create an end screen panel.
  2. Hide it at the start.
  3. Show it when the player wins.
  4. Add a restart button.
  5. Reload the scene when restart is pressed.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why should the end screen be hidden when the level starts?

docs.title: :material-loop: Lesson 18 - Complete Game Loop
docs.text: Source video: Complete Game Loop! Win Condition, End Screen, & Scene Reset

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Connect movement, collecting, scoring, winning and restarting into one complete playable loop.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can play from start to win screen.
  • I can restart after winning.
  • I can identify each part of the game loop.
  • I can fix missing connections or broken signals.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Game loop The repeated cycle of playing, achieving and restarting. Objective The goal the player tries to complete. Integration Connecting separate parts into a working whole. Regression A new change accidentally breaking old behaviour.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: order the steps in the game's loop. 10 mins Teacher demo: full playthrough and bug hunt. 10 mins Guided task: check all scene connections. 15 mins Independent task: complete and test the loop. 5 mins Plenary: one bug found and how it was fixed.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Start the game from the main scene.
  2. Move the player around the level.
  3. Collect all coins.
  4. Trigger the win screen.
  5. Restart and confirm the game resets.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What are the minimum features needed for this to count as a complete game?

docs.title: Lesson 19 - Debugging and Refinement
docs.text: Source videos: Whole playlist review

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Debug the platformer project and refine the player experience.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can test one feature at a time.
  • I can use output messages to trace bugs.
  • I can identify broken node paths or signal connections.
  • I can make one improvement to the game.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Debugging Finding and fixing problems. Trace Follow what the code is doing. Node path A reference to a node in the scene tree. Refinement Improving an existing feature.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: list common bugs from the project. 10 mins Teacher demo: debugging with print() and scene checks. 10 mins Guided task: inspect signals and node paths. 15 mins Independent task: fix and refine. 5 mins Plenary: share one debugging strategy.

docs.title: Practical Tasks
docs.text:
1. Test movement.
  2. Test jumping.
  3. Test each coin pickup.
  4. Test score and win condition.
  5. Improve one part of the player experience.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: Why should you test features separately before testing the whole game?

docs.title: Lesson 20 - Level Design and Extension
docs.text: Source videos: Whole playlist review

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Extend the basic game by improving the level layout and adding a small extra feature.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can arrange platforms and coins with intention.
  • I can balance challenge and fairness.
  • I can add one extension feature.
  • I can explain my design choices.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Level design Planning the space and challenges in a game. Difficulty How challenging the game is. Extension An added feature beyond the basic tutorial. Balance Making challenge feel fair.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: what makes a platform level fair? 10 mins Teacher demo: simple level design principles. 10 mins Guided task: sketch a better level route. 15 mins Independent task: build and test the extension. 5 mins Plenary: justify one design choice.

docs.title: Extension Ideas
docs.text:
1. Add more coins.
  2. Add a timer.
  3. Add a second level.
  4. Add a hazard that resets the player.
  5. Add sound effects for collecting coins.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: How did your extension improve the game rather than just make it bigger?

docs.title: Lesson 21 - Showcase and Export Preparation
docs.text: Source videos: Whole playlist review

docs.title: Learning Intention
docs.text: Prepare the finished Godot project for sharing, feedback and future improvement.

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
• I can complete a final playtest.
  • I can explain how my game works.
  • I can collect feedback from another player.
  • I can identify export or sharing steps.

docs.title: Keywords
docs.text: Keyword Meaning Showcase Presenting finished work to others. Feedback Comments that help improve a project. Export Build a playable version outside the editor. Version A saved stage of a project.

docs.title: 45 Minute Lesson Flow
docs.text: Time Activity 5 mins Starter: final checklist review. 10 mins Teacher demo: project polish and export overview. 10 mins Guided task: prepare a short game explanation. 15 mins Independent task: peer playtest and feedback. 5 mins Plenary: next step for the project.

docs.title: Final Checklist
docs.text:
1. The player can move.
  2. The player can jump.
  3. Coins can be collected.
  4. Score or progress updates.
  5. The win screen appears.
  6. Restart works.
  7. The level is fair to complete.

docs.title: Showcase Task
docs.text:
Write a short paragraph explaining:

  • The aim of your game.
  • The controls.
  • One technical feature you are proud of.
  • One improvement you would make next.

docs.title: Plenary Check
docs.text: What is the strongest evidence that your game is finished enough to share?
